_meta.purpose: Pranav's default responses to standard job-application form fields plus canonical bio/experience/publications content /apply can lift verbatim into free-text prompts. Copy to ~/.job_search/answers.json and fill any TODO fields.
_meta.never_commit_real_file: The real filled file (~/.job_search/answers.json) contains address/GPA/EEO and is gitignored. Only this .example.json ships in the repo.
conventions: Each field has a picked default in the string value.
Alternative options are listed alongside as _<field>_options arrays.
TODO markers mean the user must supply it before /apply can run cleanly.
Fields under 'sensitive' MUST NEVER be auto-filled by /apply — always leave blank.
The 'references' block points at the canonical source docs. When a free-text question needs a fact /apply doesn't have on hand here, it reads from these instead of inventing.

references._note: Read-only canonical sources. /apply and any answer-generating step should ground claims in these — never invent.

experience.reason_for_leaving_current: Not actively leaving; open to conversations where the scope and technical bar make the move worth it.
experience.reason_for_leaving_prior: Moved to alfred_ for founding-team scope and to work on production multi-agent systems end-to-end.

work_history.title: Founding LLM Engineer
work_history.summary: Founding-team engineer on a Techstars-backed consumer AI assistant, 5,000+ paying subscribers. Own the reliability layer of a multi-agent SMS/web/voice system: risk-scored gating (five-verdict decision layer with deterministic scoring), a from-scratch eval harness, an automated production-failure scanner, and re-architected working memory so LLM fabrication is structurally impossible (strict-ID pipeline). Built and scaled the email-rules engine — replaced per-message LLM calls with a deterministic three-stage matcher, cut per-user LLM cost ~30%. Cut email-to-SMS notification latency ~90s → ~3s (~30×) via polling → event-triggered delivery. Postgres/Supabase at production scale with RLS hardening.

work_history.title: AI Engineer
work_history.summary: Owned the end-to-end React / Node.js / MongoDB CMS powering 10–20K daily users; Redis caching, SSR, dynamic SEO pipeline. Cut API p95 latency from 1.5s to 300ms via query-plan tuning and connection pooling. Shipped an internal XGBoost churn-prediction dashboard on event-driven behavior tracking.

work_history.title: Research Software Engineer
work_history.summary: Built Python RESTful APIs with PostgreSQL handling 1M+ records; JWT auth middleware; deployed a quantized audio ML inference service at 150ms p95 (INT8, TorchScript, Docker CI/CD). Contributing author on the TeamMedAgents research line — multi-agent medical reasoning grounded in the Salas Big Five teamwork model.

publications.title: MetaRAG: A Systematic Framework for Enterprise Knowledge Retrieval
publications.one_line: Metadata-enrichment framework for RAG that beats content-only retrieval by injecting LLM-generated metadata into chunk representations. 82.5% precision, 0.925 Hit@10, 25% hallucination reduction. Deployed on AWS SageMaker, p99 <300ms, 10K queries/day.

publications.title: TeamMedAgents: Enhancing Medical Decision-Making of LLMs Through Teamwork
publications.one_line: Multi-agent LLM medical reasoning framework grounded in Salas et al.'s Big Five teamwork model; six operationalized teamwork components as toggleable mechanisms; consistent improvements on 7 of 8 medical benchmarks.

publications.title: SLM-TeamMedAgents: Scaling Medical Decision Making with SLMs
publications.one_line: Collaborative Small Language Models (500M–27B) matching frontier 100B+ models at orders-of-magnitude lower cost. Built on Google ADK with Gemma / MedGemma. 77.63% across 8 benchmarks, 3.1× inference speedup vs frontier LLMs.

typical_short_answers._note: Fallback stock answers for questions where the JD gives no specific hook. /apply should read the JD first and write something more tailored using the voice rules (job_search_apply_voice.md). These are used only when the question is completely generic.
typical_short_answers.elevator_pitch_short: I'm a founding LLM engineer at alfred_, a Techstars-backed AI assistant with 5,000+ paying users. I own the multi-agent reliability layer — a five-verdict decision system with deterministic risk scoring, an eval harness, and a working-memory pipeline I re-architected so LLM fabrication is structurally impossible. Prior to alfred_ I shipped a customer-facing CMS at WheelPrice (10–20K DAU) and I have three first-author papers in multi-agent LLM systems and RAG, including one accepted at IEEE CAI 2026.
typical_short_answers.elevator_pitch_long: I'm the founding LLM engineer at alfred_, a Techstars-backed consumer AI assistant that manages a user's email, calendar, and daily obligations over SMS, web, and voice — 5,000+ paying subscribers today. My work spans three layers most companies split across teams: agent infrastructure (multi-agent orchestration, tool-calling, a five-verdict execution decision layer with deterministic risk scoring); product core (the email-rules engine and a per-user working-memory system I re-architected into a strict-ID pipeline so LLM fabrication is structurally impossible, not just reduced); and production reliability (a deterministic eval harness, an automated production-failure scanner, LLM cost engineering that cut per-user cost ~30% on the rules path). Before alfred_ I built the React/Node/Mongo CMS at WheelPrice serving 10–20K DAU and cut API latency 1.5s → 300ms. I hold three first-author publications in multi-agent LLM systems and RAG including one accepted at IEEE CAI 2026, and I was a graduate assistant at UIC's VARE Lab.
typical_short_answers.why_engineering_ai: I like the intersection where a system has to be trustworthy, cheap, and fast at the same time. Most agentic AI work today has to make trade-offs across those three, and figuring out which axis matters for a given feature is more interesting than any single one on its own.
typical_short_answers.greatest_strength: I own vertical slices end to end — design through production and iteration on live user data. That lets me pick the right layer to fix a problem at, rather than defaulting to whichever layer I'm most comfortable with.
typical_short_answers.greatest_weakness: I over-invest in eval and observability early on a project. It slows the first couple of days, and I've had to learn when the payoff justifies it and when a scrappier approach is fine.
typical_short_answers.biggest_impact_story: The alfred_ email-to-SMS notification pipeline was arriving ~90 seconds after a user's email — a polling timer, not compute. I moved delivery to an instant event trigger the moment a notification is queued, with the old cron demoted to a backstop. Latency went to ~3 seconds, about 30×. Then I extended the same fix to the security/OTP path, which had been 189s at p90 — that went instant too. It's a small architectural change but it moved a metric a lot of users felt every day.
typical_short_answers.hardest_technical_problem: The working-memory pipeline at alfred_. An LLM summarizing an inbox will confidently invent threads, invert who owes the next move, or show a closed loop as still open. I re-architected the pipeline into a strict-ID design where the LLM only selects from a menu of real candidates behind opaque handles and writes prose — it never emits or echoes an identifier or ownership field. All identity, ownership, and closed-state data is re-attached by code. That makes a whole class of hallucination structurally impossible rather than probabilistically reduced, and it's enforced by tests.
typical_short_answers.why_leaving_current_role: Not actively leaving. I'm at a Techstars-backed startup with a live paying user base and I like my scope. I'm open to conversations where the technical bar and scope of impact make the move worth it.
typical_short_answers.five_year_goal: Keep building at the intersection of agentic systems and production reliability. Longer term, own the eval / observability / trust stack for a product that a lot of people use — whether that's my own or someone else's.

project_stories._note: Ready-to-lift answers for 'tell us about a project' style questions. Pick the one whose stack/domain matches the role.

alfred_decision_layer.one_line: A five-verdict execution decision layer for a live multi-agent AI assistant.
alfred_decision_layer.story: The alfred_ agent takes actions on a user's email, calendar, and SMS. Each candidate action needs to be gated — some are silent, some need confirmation, some need to be refused entirely. I designed a five-verdict system (SILENT / NOTIFY / CONFIRM / CLARIFY / REFUSE) using deterministic risk scoring rather than an LLM-as-judge, so the same input always produces the same verdict and the decision is auditable. It ships with a pending-obligations queue and a bounded undo window for recoverable side effects. Now runs across the production agent for 5,000+ users.

metarag.one_line: A metadata-enrichment framework for RAG accepted at IEEE CAI 2026.
metarag.story: Vector search over document chunks tends to miss on enterprise knowledge because the chunk itself doesn't carry enough context. I used an LLM to generate structured metadata per chunk (type, source, key entities, likely question types) and injected it into the embedding representation. Combined with cross-encoder reranking and TF-IDF weighted embeddings, the system hit 82.5% precision and 0.925 Hit@10 versus a 73.3% baseline, and cut hallucinations 25% via an automated evaluation framework. Deployed on AWS SageMaker with MLflow, 10K queries/day in a production code-translation use case, p99 latency under 300ms.

snakeai_mlops.one_line: A C++/LibTorch reinforcement learning system with a real Python-to-C++ inference bridge.
snakeai_mlops.story: I wanted to force myself into a real C++ systems problem. The interesting work was the integration, not the ML: getting trained PyTorch weights running inside a C++ game loop with a proper artifact contract, CI/CD, and a diagnostic layer that surfaces failures early. I built four RL algorithms (Q-Learning, DQN, PPO, Actor-Critic), a TorchScript conversion gate with shape validation, and a TORCH_AVAILABLE compile flag for graceful degradation when LibTorch is absent. Also hit a LibTorch memory-corruption bug where inference outputs degraded gradually after a few minutes — fixed by moving to a RAII pattern for the input tensor, validated with a 50,000-iteration soak test.

wheelprice_cms.one_line: A React/Node/Mongo CMS serving 10–20K daily users with a 1.5s → 300ms latency win.
wheelprice_cms.story: WheelPrice needed a content layer to capture organic search traffic. I built the CMS as an independent Node.js/React service under the same domain — SSR, dynamic SEO pipeline, Redis caching with tag-based invalidation. First version had no caching; when a social post went viral the SSR-against-Mongo path tanked. Added the Redis layer and connection pooling — API p95 went from 1.5s to 300ms, and daily views grew to 10–20K over the following months as long-tail queries started resolving.

sensitive._note: NEVER auto-fill any of these. If a form asks, /apply should skip the field and log it for manual review.
